title: Space Station Encounters
description: Events, people, and situations encountered aboard a large station or orbital — the texture of life at the intersection of deep space and civilization.

entries.result: A docking bay is sealed and guarded. No official explanation. Ships are being rerouted. The traffic controllers sound strained.
A vendor in the market district is selling items that clearly came off a ship — personal effects, not cargo. The vendor won't say where they got them.
A child is sitting alone near a docking terminal with a bag and no one who appears to be waiting for them.
Two crews from competing operations are in the same corridor and the situation is a few seconds from becoming a problem.
A station announcement plays — routine, calm — and a small group of people in the concourse react with visible alarm.
A maintenance worker flags down the party. There's something in the conduit they were servicing. They don't know what it is. They stopped working immediately.
A derelict ship has been brought in on a salvage claim and is sitting in dock. The salvage crew is being held for questioning. They look shaken.
A market stall has been abandoned mid-business — merchandise out, payment terminal on, the operator nowhere visible.
A station medic is moving through the population level quickly, taking samples from surfaces, saying nothing to passersby.
An older station resident approaches the party with specific questions about where they've recently been.
The gravity in one section is slightly off — not enough to be dangerous, enough to be noticeable. No announcement.
A runner — young, fast — weaves through the crowd and passes close enough to the party to slip something into a pocket.
A shipment has been flagged at customs and the owner is arguing loudly that the contents are exactly what was declared.
A popular food vendor has a long queue, but the people at the front are lingering and clearly talking about something.
A face on a wanted notice matches someone the party passed thirty seconds ago.
A section of the station's outer ring is visible through a viewport and it looks different than it did yesterday.
A stranger offers the party cargo space on a departing ship — cheap, no questions. They need it filled before the window closes.
Station security is interviewing people in a corridor, working through the crowd methodically.
A technician is repairing a comm panel and looks too interested in what's coming through it.
A traveler sits alone in a transit lounge with too much luggage and the look of someone who's been waiting much longer than they expected.
The lighting in a residential corridor has been flickering for what the residents say has been weeks.
A ship in dock has its airlock open but no crew visible and no activity ongoing.
Someone in a crowd passes a physical object to someone else in a way that was clearly practiced.
A new shop has opened in the market that sells things no one can determine a use for.
A station official is being escorted out of a restricted area by two people who are clearly not station staff.
An emergency drill is announced and half the station population treats it as an inconvenience and half treat it as something else.
A pilot the party recognizes from another port is here, and they shouldn't be — they were somewhere very different when last heard from.
A group of passengers from a delayed ship are using the docking concourse as a temporary camp.
A data terminal in a public area is running something it shouldn't be when the party happens to glance at it.
Someone is distributing physical pamphlets — rare, deliberate — about something the station administration would prefer not be discussed.
A maintenance hatch is open that is always supposed to be closed. The hatch accesses a section between the walls.
A trader is offering to buy a very specific category of item at well above market price, no questions asked.
A section of the station smells wrong — not life support failure, something else.
A veteran station inhabitant pulls the party aside to quietly point out something about the people who've been watching them.
A fight breaks out in a corridor and is broken up quickly. One of the combatants makes eye contact with a party member before being led away.
A public display is running information about a political situation that the station's official channels have said is resolved.
A section of the market is conspicuously empty — stalls closed, area clear — in an area that is never empty.
A ship is departing ahead of schedule and a dockworker is watching it go with a particular expression.
A stranger asks the party for a favor that is small and oddly specific.
An air recycler in the area is running loud — louder than it should for its capacity rating.
A species the party hasn't encountered before has a small delegation on the station. No one seems certain how to interact with them.
A cargo loader has been sitting in the same position in the docking bay for twelve hours with no crew and no movement.
A group of children are running through a non-residential level where children shouldn't be unattended.
A person sitting in a transit area is writing something very long by hand.
Station comms drop out briefly — two seconds, station-wide — and the reaction from the staff visible from here is not routine.
Two people having a quiet argument in a corridor shift to a completely different subject the moment they notice the party.
A ship that was due to arrive has not. The berth is empty. The crew's contacts are asking around.
A medical alert sounds in the residential section. Station medics move through the crowd. It clears quickly. No announcement follows.
A new regulation has been posted at customs — posted this morning, taking effect immediately — that affects the party's situation.
A station elder — someone who has been here since the station was built — sits in the concourse watching the traffic and is willing to talk.
A technician runs past the party toward a section that is not, as far as they know, experiencing an emergency.
A job posting is pinned to a physical board — someone still uses those — for work that is clearly dangerous and compensated accordingly.
The party's ship receives a hail from someone on the station who shouldn't know they're here.
A very old ship is in dock — not in use, not for sale, just sitting, berthed and maintained at what must be significant ongoing cost.
A section of the station that should be accessible is sealed without explanation. A small group is waiting for it to reopen.
A traveler offers to sell information about conditions at the last station they came from.
A drone hovers at eye level, watching, then moves on.
A food stall is cooking something that smells extraordinary and has a queue that wraps out of sight.
A pilot is arguing with dock authority about a departure delay. The pilot's cargo is time-sensitive in a way they won't specify.
A translation service is set up near the gate where a non-human vessel has just docked.
The station's AI offers assistance and the suggestion it makes is unexpectedly specific to the party's actual situation.
A group of workers finishing a shift are comparing notes about something that happened on their level. They go quiet when they notice outsiders.
A cargo manifest discrepancy has been flagged and the parties involved are meeting in the concourse to sort it out before it becomes official.
Someone is crying quietly in a corner of the transit lounge and the crowd is working very hard to pretend not to notice.
A section of the station's outer hull is being repaired and the work crews' conversation, overheard, suggests the damage wasn't from wear.
A newly docked crew is being interviewed by people who are definitely not customs.
A station resident recognizes someone in the party from a description they were given and is deciding what to do about it.
A betting pool is active in the crew quarters over something that will be resolved within the next few hours.
A meeting is happening in a public lounge that is clearly not a social meeting and where the participants are not trying hard enough to look casual.
An automated system apologizes for an outage that the party didn't notice — because it affected a system they weren't using, but now know exists.
A new arrival to the station asks the party for directions to somewhere they've been avoiding.
The docking fee has increased since the last time the party was here. The increase is unexplained and significant.
A section of the station is decorated differently from the rest — cultural markers from a specific group that has established a community here.
A station-side crew is preparing to depart and needs one more body for a short-haul run. The pay is good.
A dispute is being refereed by a station official in the market — a contract disagreement, loud enough that the specifics are audible.
A man at the bar is buying rounds and asking each recipient the same question.
A hazmat team passes through a residential corridor in full gear, politely but firmly moving civilians out of their way.
A warrant has been posted for someone currently on the station. The description is close to one of the party.
A data vendor is selling star charts for a sector that is officially restricted — the charts are expensive and the vendor claims they're recent.
A child tugs on a party member's sleeve and hands them something before running back to a nearby adult.
A political rally is being held in the concourse — small, contained, but the station security presence around it is disproportionate.
A ship's captain is drunk in a public area and talking about something they shouldn't.
The gravity fluctuates — briefly, once, noticeably — and the station crew's reaction is more controlled than the situation warrants.
A broker is taking private meetings in a docking lounge, seeing people one at a time, with a very short queue.
A passenger ship has just arrived from a colony world and the passengers have a look that the station's longtime residents recognize.
The station's security chief is personally present in a sector that doesn't usually require their presence.
A crew member from a recently arrived ship is looking for someone urgently, asking everyone in the concourse.
A private message has been left at the station's message relay addressed to one of the party using a name only specific people would know to use.
An old freighter crew and a new corporate crew are competing for the same cargo, and the negotiation has moved into the concourse.
A sector of the station is running on emergency power. The lights are dimmer. The people there seem used to it.
Someone is recruiting for something — not openly, but systematically working through the transient population.
A system-wide alert goes out, tests successfully, and is logged as routine. Two people standing near the party react as if it was anything but.
A pilot has parked their ship without filing a docking plan and is arguing with dock authority about why that should be acceptable.
The station's news terminal is running a story about something that, if accurate, significantly affects the party's plans.
A non-human crew has been refused service at a market stall. The situation is at the point of being resolved in any number of ways.
The station administrator is visible in the concourse — unscheduled, without an entourage — speaking to people at random.
A ship that was impounded six months ago is suddenly cleared for departure and its crew is moving quickly.
A distress call from somewhere in the system has been picked up and the station is deciding whether to respond.
A section of the station has been sealed and is listed as 'under maintenance.' Station residents say it's been 'under maintenance' for three years.
The station is quieter than it should be. Not empty — occupied. Just quiet. The people here know something the party doesn't, and they're watching to see if the party is about to find out.
